---
title: ""
author: "Erin M. Buchanan"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: night
editor: source
incremental: true 
scrollable: true 
css: "title.css"
preview-links: true
code-copy: true 
highlight-style: github 
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

## Outline {.smaller}

-   A bit on the SPAML
-   Researcher degrees of freedom
-   Does this change German priming?

## 

Big Framing Slide: “Researcher Degrees of Freedom in Mega-Studies”

Start by framing: • 25,000+ participants • 19 analyzed languages • 21+ million trials • Adaptive sampling • Item-level reliability focus

Then say:

“With a dataset this large, preprocessing and analytic decisions matter. So let me walk you through what we decided — and what we could have done differently.”

⸻

2️⃣ Stimulus Construction Decisions

Cross-linguistic stimulus matching

￼

Key decisions: • Used OpenSubtitles corpus for frequency • Used fastText word embeddings (subs2vec models) • Used cosine similarity • Selected top 5 neighbors per concept • Cross-referenced across languages • Native speaker validation • Avoided selecting in English first → then translating

Slide idea:

“Alternative multiverse options” • Different corpus (Wikipedia only?) • Different embedding model (word2vec? GloVe?) • Lemmatized vs surface forms • Different similarity threshold (\<.15 for unrelated) • Association norms instead of embeddings

You can say:

“Each of these would produce a different stimulus set — potentially a different priming distribution.”

⸻

3️⃣ Nonword Construction Decisions

Major methodological point.

Decisions: • Wuggy-like algorithm for alphabetic languages • Radical/stroke manipulation for logographic languages • Native speaker validation • Matched length and transition frequency • Avoided pseudohomophones

Multiverse angle: • What if: • We allowed pseudohomophones? • Matched bigram frequency differently? • Used pronounceability norms? • Used simpler random strings?

Nonword construction affects: • Lexical decision difficulty • RT distribution tails • Accuracy exclusions • Cross-language comparability

⸻

4️⃣ Sampling Design Decisions (Huge Strength of Study)

Adaptive AIPE sampling

￼

Decisions: • Minimum per item: 50 • Maximum per item: 320 • Stopping rule: SE ≈ 0.09 • Decreased probability once item met precision goal • Z-scored by participant session

Multiverse perspective: • Fixed N per language instead? • Fixed N per item? • Different stopping rule (SE = .06? .12?) • Target reliability (.80) instead of SE?

This is a perfect place to discuss:

“We optimized precision, not power — which is a philosophical choice.”

⸻

5️⃣ Participant-Level Exclusion Decisions

Pre-registered criteria

￼

Participant-level: • Age ≥ 18 • ≥100 trials • ≥80% accuracy

Critical deviation: • Two interpretations of accuracy formula • Strict version retained fewer participants

Multiverse slide:

Run: • 80% • 75% • 85% • Alternative accuracy formula • No accuracy exclusion

Ask:

“Does priming change meaningfully?”

This is an extremely clean multiverse axis.

⸻

6️⃣ Trial-Level Cleaning Decisions

Major researcher degrees of freedom here.

Decisions: • Removed: • Timeouts (\>3000ms) • Incorrect trials • RT \< 160ms • Z-scored within session • Tested: • Full data • \|Z\| \> 2.5 removed • \|Z\| \> 3.0 removed

Multiverse axes: • No lower bound? • 100ms instead of 160ms? • No Z trimming? • 2.0 instead of 2.5? • 99th percentile trimming? • Log-transform instead of Z-score? • Mixed-effects model at trial level instead of item-level averages?

You already have a built-in mini-multiverse: • Full • Z2.5 • Z3.0

That’s very elegant.

⸻

7️⃣ Priming Score Construction Decision

Priming defined as:

Unrelated (Z) − Related (Z)

Item-level averages.

Alternatives: • Trial-level mixed model • Participant-level priming • Median instead of mean • Raw RT difference instead of Z • Log-RT difference • Include accuracy in composite score • Drift-diffusion modeling

This is a great “hidden degrees of freedom” slide.

⸻

8️⃣ Model Specification Decisions

Hypothesis 1: • Intercept-only model • Priming \> 0

Hypothesis 2: • Add random intercept for language • Compare AIC • Conclude heterogeneity if ΔAIC ≥ 2

￼

Multiverse: • Bayesian model? • Random slopes? • Treat language as fixed effect? • Meta-analytic approach? • Hierarchical trial-level model? • Cross-classified random effects (item × language)?

You can say:

“We chose parsimony. But a richer hierarchical model might redistribute variance differently.”

⸻

9️⃣ Reliability Decisions

Item reliability: • Split-half • Spearman-Brown correction • 100 random splits

Participant reliability: • Same approach

Multiverse: • Bootstrapped reliability? • Hierarchical reliability? • ICC instead of split-half? • Reliability at language level?

This connects directly to: • Sample size per item • SE stopping rule • Interpretation of cross-language variance

⸻

🔟 Cross-Language Variability Framing

You found: • Small but non-zero language variance • Pseudo R² \< .01

Multiverse extensions: • Does variance increase with: • Orthographic depth? • Morphological complexity? • Corpus size? • Item reliability? • Script type (logographic vs alphabetic)?

This moves the talk from: “Do languages differ?” to “What explains why they differ?”

⸻

🎯 How to Present the Multiverse Section

I’d structure it like this:

Slide 1: “Researcher Degrees of Freedom”

List 6 decision layers: • Stimuli • Nonwords • Sampling • Exclusions • Priming computation • Modeling

Slide 2: “What Actually Changes the Result?”

Show: • Effect stays positive • Magnitude shifts slightly • Language variance small but stable

Then say:

“The priming effect is robust — but its size and heterogeneity are conditional on analytic decisions.”

⸻

💡 High-Level Conceptual Framing for Audience

You could close this section with: • Registered Reports constrain flexibility • But multiverse thinking reveals where flexibility still exists • Large-scale datasets amplify the impact of preprocessing choices • Transparency + shared data → enables secondary multiverse analyses

⸻

If You Want a Very Sharp Conceptual Ending Slide

Title:

“What Did We Learn About Priming — and What Did We Learn About Decisions?”

Two columns:

Priming • Robust across 19 languages • Small heterogeneity • Improved item reliability

Decisions • Exclusion rules slightly shift magnitude • Z-thresholds minimally affect conclusions • Stimulus selection is a massive hidden decision • Modeling framework shapes interpretation of variability

⸻

If you’d like, I can also: • Draft a 5-minute version • Draft a 15-minute version • Help you turn this into slides • Or help you design an actual small multiverse re-analysis plan for a follow-up paper

## paper

![<https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0010945223002964-mmc3.pdf>](images/clipboard-2667185129.png)

<https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0010945223002964-mmc4.pdf>

![](images/clipboard-3980866384.png)

## What we did

+-----------------+--------------+------------------+------------+
|                 | Participants | Trials           | Stimuli    |
+=================+==============+==================+============+
| External Events |              | Duplicate trials | Miscodings |
+-----------------+--------------+------------------+------------+
| Fixed           | 18 years     | \< 160 ms        |            |
|                 |              |                  |            |
|                 | 80% accuracy | \> 3000 ms       |            |
|                 |              |                  |            |
|                 | 100 Trials   | Correct          |            |
+-----------------+--------------+------------------+------------+
| Data            |              | Z-score 2.5      |            |
|                 |              |                  |            |
|                 |              | Z-score 3.0      |            |
+-----------------+--------------+------------------+------------+

## What we could do

note calculated on the full data each criteria and then removed on each stage to avoid order effects

| Type         | Explanation         | Options                       |
|--------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| Participants | No exclusion\*      | None\*                        |
| Participants | Age\*               | 18 years\*                    |
| Participants | Accuracy            | none, 75%, 80%, 85%           |
| Participants | Number of Trials    | none, 50, 100                 |
| Participants | Performance cut off | 5%, 10% performers            |
| Participants | RT cut off          | Lowest RT variability 5%, 10% |

## Trials

| Type   | Explanation    | Options             |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Trials | No exclusion   | None                |
| Trials | Lower Bound RT | None, 100, 160, 200 |
| Trials | Upper Bound RT | None\*, 3000, 2500  |
| Trials | Z-Score        | None, 2.5, 3.0      |
| Trials | Lower Bound    | 5%, 10% trimming    |
| Trials | Upper Bound    | 5%, 10% trimming    |

## Stimuli

| Type    | Explanation          | Options          |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| Stimuli | No exclusion         | None             |
| Stimuli | Accuracy             | None, 25%, 50%   |
| Stimuli | Sample size          | None, 30, 50     |
| Stimuli | Lower Bound Accuracy | 5%, 10% trimming |
